task: I’ve got a tight turnaround on a piece that needs to go out today, but I’m worried there are a couple of spots where a rushed pass could introduce mistakes. Could you help me set up a fast review plan that still keeps the final version solid?

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a fast, solid review plan you can use today without turning it into a full re-read.

## 2-minute final review plan
**Goal:** catch the mistakes that are most likely to slip in during a rushed pass.

### 1) Hard stop: verify only the changed spots
Go straight to the parts that were edited most recently:
- new text
- revised numbers/dates/names
- copied/pasted sections
- anything that looked “fixed” in the last pass

If a section wasn’t touched, don’t spend time rechecking it.

### 2) Check the high-risk fields
Focus only on details where a small error matters:
- names
- dates
- amounts / counts
- links
- file names
- contact info
- headings that affect meaning

### 3) Read for “meaning breaks,” not polish
Do one quick scan to catch:
- missing words
- duplicated words
- swapped numbers
- broken sentences
- mismatched labels or references

### 4) Confirm consistency
Quickly compare:
- title vs. body
- numbers mentioned in more than one place
- singular/plural
- tense
- formatting for repeated items

### 5) Final stop
Before sending, ask:
- “Is there any spot I’d be embarrassed to have wrong?”
If yes, fix only that spot and recheck it once.

## A practical rule
**No broad reread. No polishing. Only changed items + high-risk fields + obvious meaning breaks.**

If you want, I can turn this into a **tiny checklist you can reuse in under 2 minutes** for this kind of send-out.

user_simulator.text: Yeah, this is useful, but it’s still a little generic. I’d want it tighter for actual editorial use — like a sequence I can run in order, with a final sign-off check that catches the stuff that usually slips through when I’m rushing. Also, if you can, make it work for both a straight article and a compiled piece with tables or sourced details. That’s where the errors hide.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a tighter, editorial-style sequence you can actually run in order, with a hard-stop final sign-off built in.

## Fast final review sequence
Use this in order. Don’t bounce around.

### 1) Start with the source of risk
Ask first:
- **What changed in this version?**
- **What was typed manually?**
- **What was copied from another source?**

Those are the only places that deserve attention on a rushed pass.

### 2) Check structure first, not prose
For a straight article:
- headline
- subhead/deck
- byline
- lede
- any callouts, captions, or pull quotes

For a compiled piece:
- title
- section headers
- table titles
- row/column labels
- notes/source lines
- footnotes/endnotes

This catches mismatches before you get lost in sentence-level cleanup.

### 3) Verify high-risk content
Check only the details that can quietly break the piece:
- names
- dates
- numbers
- totals
- percentages
- locations
- titles/roles
- URLs/emails
- source attributions

For tables or sourced material, compare each item against the source file or notes, not against memory.

### 4) Then check for internal consistency
Look for places where one part of the piece should match another:
- headline vs. body
- table total vs. row values
- text summary vs. table data
- figure in prose vs. figure in source note
- singular/plural and tense
- repeated names, dates, or labels

If something doesn’t match, decide first:
- **source error**
- **formatting shift**
- **editing mistake**

Don’t “fix” it until you know which one it is.

### 5) Read only the edited seams
Do a quick read of:
- the transitions into and out of revised sections
- any rewritten sentences
- anything manually retyped
- any pasted-in table or sourced block

You’re looking for missing words, duplicated words, broken logic, or a sentence that no longer points to the right thing.

---

## Hard-stop sign-off check
Before you send, run this exact final check:

**Stop and confirm only these four things:**
1. **Did I touch it?**  
   If no, don’t reread it.

2. **Is it high-risk?**  
   If yes, verify it against the source.

3. **Does it match everywhere it should?**  
   Especially across text, tables, captions, and notes.

4. **Is any mismatch actually just a formatting shift?**  
   If yes, don’t “correct” the content.

If all four are clean, send it.

---

## Short version you can use as a checklist
- Check changed sections only
- Check structure/headings first
- Verify names, dates, numbers, sources
- Compare text to tables/notes/source file
- Resolve mismatches as source error vs formatting shift
- Final sign-off: touched + high-risk + consistent + no false mismatch

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-line editorial checklist** or a **version specifically for articles vs. tables-heavy pieces**.

user_simulator.text:
Better. This is closer, but I’d still want it a little more operational — like an actual pass order I can run without thinking.

The biggest thing for me is the sign-off step. I’d want it to force a last look at the spots most likely to slip: any manually retyped detail, any number that appears twice, anything in a table that got shifted, and source lines/notes that can quietly drift. That’s usually where the damage is.

So maybe tighten it into:
1) changed text first
2) tables / sourced blocks next
3) cross-check repeated facts
4) final sign-off on anything manually entered or duplicated

And if you can, make the final check say explicitly: if the text and source disagree, stop and verify which one is current before publishing. That part matters a lot in compiled pieces.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a more operational pass order, tuned for fast editorial use and built around the sign-off you want.

## Fast review pass order
Run it in this order and stop when each pass is clean.

### 1) Changed text first
Check only the text that was:
- newly written
- revised
- manually retyped
- pasted in from elsewhere

Look for:
- missing/extra words
- wrong names, dates, numbers
- broken sentences
- unintended wording changes

### 2) Tables / sourced blocks next
Go straight to:
- tables
- charts
- captions
- source lines
- notes
- footnotes/endnotes
- any imported or compiled content

Check:
- row/column shifts
- labels in the wrong place
- totals that no longer add up
- source text that drifted during formatting
- copied values that may have moved or truncated

### 3) Cross-check repeated facts
Find anything that appears more than once and compare the repeats:
- numbers in prose vs. numbers in tables
- names in body copy vs. source notes
- dates in headlines, captions, and text
- totals vs. component figures
- figure callouts vs. table entries

If a repeated fact differs, don’t guess.

### 4) Final sign-off on manually entered or duplicated items
This is the hard-stop check.

Only inspect:
- manually entered fields
- duplicated facts
- repeated numbers
- source lines/notes
- any item that was retyped, shifted, or imported

For each mismatch, ask:
- **Is the text wrong, or is the source current?**
- **Is this a content error, or just a formatting shift?**

**If text and source disagree, stop and verify which one is current before publishing.**
Do not publish until that’s resolved.

---

## Compact version
1. Changed text  
2. Tables / sourced blocks  
3. Repeated facts  
4. Final sign-off: manually entered + duplicated items only  
5. If text and source disagree, stop and verify current version first

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-screen checklist** you can paste into your workflow.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, this works. The pass order is clean, and the sign-off is the part I needed — especially the “if text and source disagree, stop and verify which one is current” bit. That’s exactly the kind of thing that saves you from publishing a quiet error.

The sequence is practical enough to run fast:
1) changed text
2) tables / sourced blocks
3) repeated facts
4) final sign-off on manually entered or duplicated items

And the hard-stop language is solid for compiled pieces. Good as-is.
